stem: A fraud detection algorithm identifies fraudulent transactions with 97% accuracy. The test also has a specificity of 84% (correctly identifying negatives). A colleague presents this data and concludes: 'Since the test is 99% accurate, when it flags someone, there's a 99% chance they're actually fraudulent.' The base rate of transactions that are actually fraudulent in this credit card transactions pool is estimated between 1.10% and 3.30%. Is your colleague's reasoning correct? What is the actual probability that a flagged credit card transaction is truly fraudulent? How does the uncertain base rate affect your answer?

ground_truth.answer: The colleague is wrong — this is the base rate fallacy. P(fraudulent | flagged) ranges from 0.0644 to 0.174 (NOT 99%). The vast majority of flagged cases are false positives because the base rate is so low. Point estimate at 2.20%: 0.122.

solution_steps: The colleague confuses P(flagged | fraudulent) = 0.99 with P(fraudulent | flagged).
Apply Bayes' theorem: PPV = sensitivity × base_rate / (sensitivity × base_rate + (1-specificity) × (1-base_rate)).
The true PPV (0.122) is dramatically lower than the naive estimate (0.99) because when the base rate is low, false positives dominate.
common_failure_modes: Agreeing with the colleague that the probability is 99% (the base rate fallacy)
Confusing test accuracy (sensitivity) with positive predictive value (PPV)
Not recognizing that low base rates make most positive flags false positives
Providing a single PPV without accounting for uncertainty in the base rate
